properties.description: "We hold these truths to be self-evident..." So began Thomas Jefferson's audacious preamble to the Declaration of Independence, ratified unanimously on July 4. The Thirteen Colonies were separating from Britain.
The Continental Army, led by Horatio Gates, decisively defeated the British in one of the turning points of the Revolutionary War, convincing France to enter the war as an American ally.
In the last battle of the British Army's Philadelphia campaign, the Americans proved their mettle against professionals following intensive winter training at Valley Forge.
American and French forces failed to retake this important port, resulting in heavy casualties and solidifying British control of the South.
A significant British victory and the worst American defeat of the war, resulting in the surrender of thousands of Continental troops.
British troops led by Charles Cornwallis surrendered to George Washington and his French allies, securing American victory in the Revolutionary War.
Pennsylvania militamen rounded up and killed 96 pacifist Moravian Christian Indians, mainly of the Lenape and Mohican tribes, in one of the most horrifying instances of frontier violence.

In one of the great acts of statesmanship in American history, George Washington resigned as commander-in-chief of the Continental Army, establishing the critical precedent of civilian control over the military.

Delegates from four North Carolina counties, dissatisfied with their state government, declared their independence. The Franklin experiment, named to get Benjamin Franklin on board, lasted just four years.

Founding Father Abraham Baldwin had his charter approved by the Georgia General Assembly in Savannah, establishing the University of Georgia as the first state-chartered public university in the United States.

Protesters in Western Massachusetts launched an armed uprising against the state's increased efforts to collect taxes and debts, highlighting the weaknesses of the federal government under the Articles of Confederation.
From May 25 to September 17, the Founding Fathers gathered in Philadelphia to create the Constitution of the United States, an enduring document which remains the law of the land.
New Hampshire became the ninth state to ratify, making the U.S. Constitution the official law of the land.
On the balcony of Federal Hall, George Washington took the presidential oath of office, becoming the first President of the United States.

Samuel Slater's water-powered mill began operating on the banks of the Blackstone River. Slater would go on to build several successful cotton mills, helping turn New England into a hub of the textile industry.
Following a 105-2 vote by the Vermont General Assembly in Bennington, the independent Vermont Republic became the first state admitted to the Union after the original 13 colonies.
According to legend, 24 stockbrokers signed an agreement under a buttonwood tree at 68 Wall Street, marking the formal beginning of the New York Stock Exchange.

5,000 people—10% of Philadelphia's population at the time—died in a devastating yellow fever epidemic in America's temporary capital. The likely source of the virus was ships carrying French colonial refugees, some slaveowners, fleeing the Haitian Revolution.
On July 16, an armed militia of farmers surrounded the home of tax inspector John Neville to protest a tax on whiskey. The insurgency was suppressed by George Washington's troops.
The Wyandot, Delaware, and other indigenous peoples ceded most of modern-day Ohio to the United States, spelling the end of the American frontier in the East.

After 20 years of public service to the United States, Washington published a letter to announce his retirement, warning Americans against the dangers of political factions and permanent foreign influence.
One of the original six naval frigates, "Old Ironsides" became a symbol of American naval resilience and remained undefeated in battle.
The U.S. Navy captured its first ship: a French schooner seized during the "Quasi-War," a conflict triggered by the United States' failure to repay French loans incurred during the Revolutionary War.

A group of Pennsylvania Dutch farmers revolted against a new federal property tax. On March 7, auctioneer John Fries led armed men to free prisoners who had been arrested for refusing to obey the law.
John Adams became the first president to reside in the executive mansion, marking the formal move of the government to Washington.
Over 10,000 people gathered at the Cane Ridge Meeting House in what became the biggest event of the Second Great Awakening, a religious revival that caused American church membership to soar.
President Thomas Jefferson signed a bill establishing the U.S. Military Academy, educating cadets to serve as officers in the U.S. Army.

The American flag was raised in New Orleans' Jackson Square on December 20 as the U.S. took possession of 828,000 square miles from France, in return for less than $400 million in today's money. As a result, the United States nearly doubled in size and crossed the Mississippi River.
Vice President Aaron Burr fatally shot former Treasury Secretary Alexander Hamilton, ending a bitter rivalry between the two politicians.

properties.event: Lewis and Clark Expedition sights the Pacific
properties.description: Nineteen months after setting off from Missouri, an expedition led by Meriwether Lewis and William Clark sighted the Pacific Ocean for the first time, setting the stage for America's pioneer age.
Noah Webster published A Compendious Dictionary of the English Language, beginning his project to standardize American English. This was the first ancestor of the modern Merriam-Webster dictionary.
The North River Steamboat arrived in Albany two days after setting off from New York City, revolutionizing transportation and trade by making upstream travel efficient.
The Osage Nation ceded much of modern-day Missouri and Arkansas to the United States in what was one of the first major treaties in the Louisiana Purchase.
A group of Native Americans led by the Lenape ceded land in Illinois and Indiana to settlers, leading Shawnee chief Tecumseh to begin a war of resistance against the United States.
Armed rebels organized by Philemon Thomas stormed Fort San Carlos in Baton Rouge, declaring independence from Spain. Their territory joined the state of Louisiana in 1812.

U.S. victory against Tecumseh's confederacy of tribes gave Indiana governor William Henry Harrison a nickname that stuck, later turning into a song that would help land Harrison the presidency in 1840.

British forces, in collaboration with Shawnee leader Tecumseh's confederation, successfully deceived William Hull into believing his troops were undermanned. Hull surrendered Detroit to the British, a disaster for America in the war of 1812.
The U.S. Navy decisively defeated the British Royal Navy, ensuring American control of Lake Erie for the rest of the War of 1812.
British troops led by Robert Ross marched on the American capital, setting fire to the White House and the Capitol in retaliation for American actions in British Canada.

Despite the formal end of the war 15 days prior, American troops led by Andrew Jackson thoroughly defeated the British in the last major battle of the War of 1812. The famous victory made Jackson an American hero and was celebrated as a federal holiday until 1861.

Just two weeks before the summer solstice, the Vermont town of Cabot reported an astonishing 18 inches of snow. Much of the United States and Europe experienced extremely unusual weather throughout the "Year Without a Summer" that was 1816, due to the 1815 eruption of Mount Tambora in present-day Indonesia.
Peale's Gas Company lit up the corner of present-day Baltimore and Holliday Streets, making Baltimore the first American city with gas street lights.

properties.event: Cumberland Road reaches the Ohio River
properties.description: The first major federally funded highway reached the Ohio River, serving as a vital gateway for western expansion and trade.
Bound for Liverpool, the SS Savannah set sail on May 24 and arrived in England on June 20, making her the first steamship to cross the Atlantic Ocean.

The Missouri Compromise, which admitted Missouri as a slave state and Maine as a free state, set the stage for future conflict over the issue of slavery in America. On May 31, Maine held its first legislative session as an independent state, where slavery was prohibited.
William Becknell found a convenient trade route from Franklin, Missouri to Santa Fe, New Mexico, opening up a route that would be used by traders and emigrants alike for decades.
A free Black man and local leader was executed for planning what would have been the largest slave revolt in U.S. history.
As the American frontier moved ever westward, the U.S. Army fought its first war west of the Missouri River in response to an Arikara attack on fur traders. Neither side could claim victory.
Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute was established as the first technological university in the English-speaking world, advancing science and engineering in America.

The Erie Canal connected the upper Great Lakes to the Atlantic Ocean, accelerating settlement of the Midwest and turning New York City into a hub of international trade. Upon completion on October 26, a series of cannons were fired from Buffalo to New York.
Often cited as America's first commercial railroad, the Granite Railway was built to haul granite for the Bunker Hill Monument.

properties.event: Chartering of the Baltimore and Ohio Railroad
properties.description: The first common carrier railroad chartered in the United States was designed to compete with canals for western trade, offering an alternative to the Erie Canal route.
As the story goes, Benjamin Parks caught a glint of a gold in a rock in the woods on October 27, bringing thousands to the mountains of northern Georgia, hoping to strike it rich.
A mob of mainly Irish men attacked Black residents in their homes and on the streets of Cincinnati, forcing over half of the city's Black population out, many to Canada.

This treaty was the first under the Indian Removal Act, ceding Choctaw land in exchange for territory in modern-day Oklahoma. The Choctaw who remained in Mississippi became the first non-ethnic European U.S. citizens.
Over the course of three days, Nat Turner led a slave rebellion that killed between 55 and 65 White people across Southampton County. Turner and seventeen others were later executed.

Despite Black Hawk's attempts to surrender, U.S. forces killed 150 Sauk and Meskwaki people in the final battle of the Black Hawk War, opening up much of modern-day Illinois and Wisconsin to settlement.

56 delegates from across Mexican Texas gathered in San Felipe de Austin to draft a series of petitions, which Stephen F. Austin later presented to the government in Mexico City. Austin was imprisoned, but many of the settlers' demands were met.
An anti-Catholic mob burned down a convent, reflecting the intense nativist and religious tensions of the era.
Seminole victory over the U.S. Army following the American annexation of Spanish Florida sparked a seven-year war.

In a pivotal battle of the Texas Revolution, the deaths of James Bowie, William B. Travis, and Davy Crockett inspired many to "Remember the Alamo" and join the Texian Army, winning independence from Mexico.
Journalist and newspaper editor Elijah Lovejoy was shot and killed by a pro-slavery mob for his abolitionist views, becoming a martyr for the cause and for freedom of speech.
On May 26, the U.S. Army began rounding up over 15,000 Cherokee from their native lands, one of several forced displacements of Native Americans during the so-called Trail of Tears.

53 African captives, enslaved in Cuba, seized control of the Spanish schooner La Amistad before it was captured off the coast of Long Island. A major legal battle ensued over whether the men were free people or property; the Supreme Court ruled in favor of the Africans' freedom.
At least 317 people were killed in one of the deadliest tornadoes in American history, tracing a path of destruction across Mississippi and Louisiana.
John Bartleson and John Bidwell led the first successful contingent of emigrants on the California Trail, hoping to start new lives in the West.
Thomas Wilson Dorr sought to abolish Rhode Island's nearly 200-year-old requirement of land ownership to vote, leading his followers to storm the state arsenal in Providence.
Over 1,000 men, women, and children set out from Independence on the Oregon Trail, making a five-month journey that would unleash a wave of pioneers heading west.

Sitting in Baltimore's Mount Clare Station, Alfred Vail received the message from collaborator Samuel Morse at the U.S. Capitol: "WHAT HATH GOD WROUGHT". It was the first public demonstration of the electrical telegraph, which would revolutionize long-distance communication.
At a convention in Austin, Republic of Texas leaders voted to join the United States, leading to its admission as the 28th state and sparking conflict with Mexico.
In the first major engagement of the Mexican-American War, U.S. artillery proved decisive against Mexican forces.

"This is the right place, drive on," were the famous words uttered by Mormon leader Brigham Young upon first viewing the Salt Lake Valley. July 24 is still celebrated as Pioneer Day in Utah, which remains plurality-Mormon.

Previously a carpenter in New Jersey, James Wilson Marshall unknowingly set off a 300,000-person mass migration when he found flakes of gold in the South Fork American River at Sutter's Mill. Within three years, the "forty-niners" turned California into a U.S. state.

The first steamship to bring gold-seekers from the East Coast to San Francisco via Panama, the SS California fueled the rapid growth of the city. In the coming months, thousands of passengers would arrive in San Francisco by sea.

Over 1,000 attendees convened in Worcester to formalize a movement for women's equality, drawing national attention to the issue of women's suffrage following the Seneca Falls Convention two years prior. It would not be until 1920 that women would gain the right to vote across the country.
A group of Black and white residents successfully fought off a slaveowner attempting to capture four fugitives under the Fugitive Slave Act.
Harriet Beecher Stowe's influential anti-slavery novel galvanized Northern sentiment against slavery and fueled the abolitionist movement.

properties.event: Olympia declared the capital of Washington
properties.description: Upon the creation of the Washington Territory, Governor Isaac Stevens named Olympia the administrative center for the new region.

In May, President Franklin Pierce signed the Kansas-Nebraska Act, allowing Kansas's population to choose whether slavery would be legal. In response, so called "Free-Staters," mainly abolitionists from New England, settled in Kansas, hoping to prevent it from becoming a slave state.
A pro-slavery settler shot and killed an anti-slavery settler, escalating into an armed standoff over slavery that became part of a cycle of violence called "Bleeding Kansas."

Abolitionist John Brown orchestrated the killing of five pro-slavery settlers in response to the sacking of the city of Lawrence by pro-slavery forces, in the most famous event of the "Bleeding Kansas" period of civil war in the territory.
A militia composed of Mormons and Southern Paiute Native Americans killed over 120 people, mainly immigrant families on their way from Arkansas to California.

Republican Abraham Lincoln and Democrat Stephen Douglas, then candidates for the Illinois Senate, engaged in a series of seven debates on the expansion of slavery in U.S. territories, the first political debates to achieve national coverage.

properties.event: John Brown's raid on Harpers Ferry
properties.description: Prominent abolitionist John Brown led a raid on the Harpers Ferry Armory in an attempt to start an armed slave revolt. Brown's party was defeated, and he became the first person in American history to be executed for treason.

An express mail service using relays of horse-mounted riders was founded to reduce messaging time across the United States to just ten days. As a sign of the nation's rapid pace of integration in this era, the service was replaced the very next year by the first transcontinental telegraph.
The militia of South Carolina, now part of the Confederate States of America, fired on a U.S. Army fort, starting the Civil War.

22,727 casualties: this was the final tally from the bloodiest day in American history, as the Union army turned back Robert E. Lee's invasion of Maryland. Victory at Antietam gave President Abraham Lincoln the political capital to issue 1863's Emancipation Proclamation, which legally freed slaves in the Confederate states.

After three days of battle, with both sides suffering tens of thousands of casualties, Confederate troops were forced to retreat from the North, turning the Civil War in the Union's favor. President Abraham Lincoln traveled to the battle site four and a half months later to deliver his famous Gettysburg Address.

properties.event: Sherman's March to the Sea begins
properties.description: Union General William Tecumseh Sherman began a "scorched earth" campaign to break the Confederacy's will to fight, leaving a trail of destruction from Atlanta to Savannah.
General Robert E. Lee surrendered to Ulysses S. Grant following defeat at Appomattox, effectively ending the Civil War and beginning the era of Reconstruction.
Mobs of white residents and policemen attacked Black residents over the course of a three-day rampage, highlighting the difficulties of protecting freedmen following the Civil War.
The U.S. government signed treaties with the Kiowa, Comanche, Apache, Southern Cheyenne, and Arapaho tribes, intending to move them from settlement areas in the Great Plains to present-day Oklahoma.

Subtribes of the Lakota, Dakota, and Arapaho peoples signed a treaty with the U.S. government after a previous attempt to protect Indian territory from settlement was violated. The new treaty would be violated as well, as the government illegally took Indian land in the Black Hills.

The Central Pacific Railroad and the Union Pacific Railroad met for the first time at Promontory Summit, marked with a "golden spike" completing the first transcontinental railroad across the United States. The American West was brought into alignment with the rest of the country.
The world's first boardwalk opened in this New Jersey seaside resort city, where it remains the longest and busiest boardwalk anywhere in the world.
A massive blaze destroyed thousands of buildings and left nearly 100,000 homeless, leading to the rapid rebuilding and modernization of the city.

Congress established Yellowstone as the world's first national park, setting a global precedent for wilderness preservation. Over 150 years later, the park is still famed for its Old Faithful geyser, landscape, and wildlife.

The Colfax massacre was one of the most violent events of the post-Civil War Reconstruction, as a mob of former Confederate soldiers and Ku Klux Klan members killed dozens of Black freedmen following a disputed election. Voter intimidation would effectively end Reconstruction in Louisiana.
Lt. Col. George Armstrong Custer led an expedition to the unmapped Black Hills of South Dakota, triggering a gold rush that encroached on lands designated by treaty to the Sioux.
"The Greatest Two Minutes in Sports" was run for the first time in 1875 at Churchill Downs, making it the oldest continuously-running sporting event in the United States.

Commonly known as "Custer's Last Stand," this engagement during the Great Sioux War was a massive defeat for the U.S. Army as they were overpowered by Indian forces led by Sitting Bull, Crazy Horse, and others. 100 years since independence, the battle for American land was still ongoing.

Tens of thousands of railroad workers went on the first major nationwide strike in U.S. history after the Baltimore and Ohio Railroad cut wages, resulting in violent clashes between workers and troops.

Following Alexander Graham's Bell successful patent for the telephone, George W. Coy established the world's first telephone exchange, allowing multiple users to communicate and launching the telecommunications revolution.
Thomas Edison successfully tested a durable carbon-filament incandescent bulb, paving the way for the widespread use of electric light.
More than 10,000 people watched as four 3,000-candle power lamps were lit from the county courthouse, allowing Wabash to claim the title of the world's first electrically lighted city.
A legendary 30-second shootout between lawmen (the Earps and Doc Holliday) and the "Cowboys" became a defining moment of the Old West.

Adapting Thomas Edison's system for generating electricity, the Vulcan Street Plant in the Appleton Pulp Mill demonstrated the potential of water power. By 1900, hydroelectric power accounted for over 40% of the American electricity supply.

Showman William F. Cody, better known as "Buffalo Bill," had the first public performance of his frontier show in Omaha, a resounding success that established a lasting image of the American frontier, featuring cowboys, Native Americans, and sharpshooters.
The Washington Monument, an iconic granite obelisk commemorating America's first president, topped out at 555 feet, making it the world's tallest structure at the time.
Tensions between European and Chinese immigrants over mining jobs in the West came to a head when a mob killed at least 28 Chinese miners.
A labor protest in support of an eight-hour workday turned violent after a bomb was thrown at police. The event became a symbol of the international struggle for workers' rights.

Armed insurgents seeking the annexation of Hawaii by the United States forced King Kalākaua to sign a constitution that stripped the Hawaiian monarchy of power and disenfranchised many Native Hawaiians.
One of the deadliest winter storms ever struck the Great Plains, but in Mira Valley, a young teacher named Minnie Freeman led 13 children to safety, becoming a national hero.
At high noon on April 22, "boomers" and "sooners" made a mad dash for two million acres in present-day Oklahoma, which had just been opened to settlement.
U.S. troops killed nearly 300 Lakota people, marking the final major armed conflict between the U.S. government and Plains tribes.

A mob lynched eleven Italian immigrants following the acquittal of several men accused of murdering the city's police chief, David Hennessy. Anti-Italian sentiment intensified, bringing the word "mafia" into the American lexicon.
In a major turning point in American labor history, striking steelworkers defeated private security agents but were set back in their efforts to unionize.

A massive world's fair was held in Chicago from May 1 to October 31 to celebrate the 400th anniversary of Christopher Columbus's arrival in the New World. American industrial progress was on display, from the world's first Ferris wheel to alternating current.
In the first significant protest march on the nation's capital, unemployed workers traveled over 400 miles to demand government job creation following the Panic of 1893.

Booker T. Washington, the preeminent African-American leader of his time, delivered a speech on race relations to a predominantly white audience in Atlanta. He proposed the "Atlanta Compromise," in which Black Southerners would accept segregation in exchange for economic opportunity.

Stemming from a case filed by a mixed-race man who boarded a whites-only train car, the Supreme Court upheld "separate but equal" racial segregation, providing legal justification for Jim Crow laws for decades.
News of gold in the Yukon triggered a massive migration of 100,000 prospectors through Seattle toward the Canadian north.
During the Spanish-American War, American ships attacked the Puerto Rican capital to weaken the Spanish defense, ultimately resulting in the U.S. takeover of the island.
One of the deadliest and most destructive hurricanes in history struck Puerto Rico, killing over 3,000 and devastating the island's coffee industry.
The deadliest natural disaster in U.S. history, a massive Category 4 hurricane and storm surge utterly destroyed the booming port city of Galveston and killed an estimated 6,000 to 12,000 people.
The discovery of a massive oil gusher launched the modern petroleum industry, turning the United States into the world's leading oil producer and establishing Texas as a global energy hub.
Tally's Electric Theatre opened, becoming the first venue dedicated exclusively to showing motion pictures. Tickets started out at 10 cents a pop!
Orville and Wilbur Wright achieved the world's first successful powered, controlled, and sustained flight of a heavier-than-air aircraft, the Wright Flyer.

An international exposition was held to celebrate the centennial of the Louisiana Purchase, showcasing new technologies like the X-ray and wireless telegraph and attracting nearly 20 million people to St. Louis.

President Theodore Roosevelt became the first American to win the Nobel Peace Prize for brokering the treaty that ended the Russo-Japanese War, highlighting the rising role of the United States as a power player in diplomacy.
The deadliest earthquake in American history struck the coast of Northern California, destroying nearly all of San Francisco. The city would swiftly rebuild.
In the worst mining accident in American history, an explosion killed at least 362 Fairmont Coal Company miners, nearly half of whom were Italian migrants.
The introduction of an affordable, mass-produced car revolutionized transportation and the American industrial landscape.

properties.event: First race at the Indianapolis Motor Speedway
properties.description: Austrian racing driver Louis Schwitzer won the first race ever held at the Indianapolis Motor Speedway, a five-mile race for which tickets topped out at $1! Today, the circuit is best known for the Indy 500, held every May.
A massive wildfire burned millions of acres in Idaho and Montana, shaping modern U.S. Forest Service fire policy.
A tragic fire at a garment factory in Manhattan killed 146 workers, leading to major reforms in building safety and labor laws.
A successful textile strike was led largely by Italian immigrant women who demanded both fair wages ("bread") and a higher quality of life ("roses").

William "Pa" Klann was inspired by a visit to a Chicago slaughterhouse, giving him an idea that turned into Henry Ford's assembly line. Production time for a Model T was reduced from over 12 hours to just 93 minutes!

During a labor uprising known as the Colorado Coalfield War, National Guardsmen and private guards attacked a tent colony of striking miners, killing mainly women and children and leading to national public outrage.

A silent historical fiction drama centered around the assassination of Abraham Lincoln, The Birth of a Nation was the biggest movie of its time and a pioneer of film techniques like closeups and fadeouts. At the same time, it is remembered for its racist depiction of African-Americans and its glorification of the Ku Klux Klan.
Mexican revolutionary Pancho Villa led a raid on the small border town of Columbus, resulting in the U.S. military's Punitive Expedition into Mexico.

properties.event: First U.S. shots of World War I
properties.description: Shortly after formally entering World War I, U.S. forces in Guam fired on the German SMS Cormoran, marking America's first combat action of the war.
A case of the influenza A virus documented in rural Kansas would turn into a global pandemic that killed tens of millions of people.
Over 60,000 workers participated in the first major city-wide general strike in American history, a five-day work stoppage inspired in part by the 1917 Russian Revolution.
Pittsburgh-area radio station KDKA made America's first commercial broadcast on Election Day, announcing Warren G. Harding's victory before people could read about it in the newspaper.
Mobs of white supremacists rampaged through Tulsa's prosperous "Black Wall Street," destroying thousands of homes and killing hundreds of Black residents.

Interior Secretary Albert B. Fall authorized U.S. Marines to occupy the Teapot Dome oilfield to protect a lease he had illegally granted without competitive bidding. For his role in the greatest political scandal until Watergate, Fall became the first cabinet member sentenced to prison.
Fiddlin' John Carson recorded "The Little Old Log Cabin in the Lane," launching the commercial country music industry.
A team of U.S. Army Air Service aviators completed the first aerial circumnavigation of the world when they returned to Seattle's Sand Point Airfield, after 175 days and 26,345 miles traveled.

Tennessee high school teacher John T. Scopes was accused of violating Tennessee state law for teaching human evolution. The trial was the first in American history to be nationally broadcast by radio, and it reflected the clash between modern science and traditional religion at the time.

Robert H. Goddard successfully launched the world's first liquid-fueled rocket, using liquid oxygen and gasoline as propellants. The 2.5-second flight laid the foundation for modern space exploration.

properties.event: First solo crossing of the Atlantic
properties.description: Charles Lindbergh flew the Spirit of St. Louis from Long Island to Paris, a 33-hour journey that turned him into an international celebrity.
The deadliest disaster in Florida's history occurred when a hurricane caused Lake Okeechobee to overflow, killing 2,500.
The "Roaring Twenties" ended with the biggest stock market crash in American history, marking the beginning of the Great Depression. It would be another decade before the country recovered.

23-year-old Clyde Tombaugh was assigned the task of locating the theoretical "Planet X," which he discovered using a blink comparator. Pluto was considered the ninth planet until 2006, when it had its status revoked by the International Astronomical Union.

Standing 1,250 feet tall, the Empire State Building was built in just thirteen and a half months, becoming the tallest building in the world at the time and an enduring symbol of American architectural ambition during the Great Depression.
The 20-month-old son of Charles and Anne Morrow Lindbergh was abducted from the family home and murdered, in what became known as the "crime of the century."
Utah's state ratifying convention provided the 36th and final vote needed to repeal the 18th Amendment, which had prohibited alcohol nationwide.

Bonnie and Clyde became nationally famous for their string of bank robberies and murders, but the jig was up for the notorious outlaw couple when they were ambushed by Texas and Louisiana officers on Highway 154.
The most severe dust storm of the Dust Bowl era turned day into night across the Plains, causing massive agricultural damage across the Great Plains.

A feat of American ingenuity and engineering, the Hoover Dam created Lake Mead, transformed the Colorado River into a major source of irrigation water and hydroelectric power, and helped make Las Vegas the city it is today.
A rigid airship filled with hydrogen burst into flames while docking, killing 36 people and effectively ending the era of commercial passenger airships.

Orson Welles' radio adaptation of the H.G. Wells novel about a Martian invasion caused widespread panic among listeners who thought it was a news report, especially in Grovers Mill, the fictional landing site.

The tale of an epic romance set in the South during the Civil War and Reconstruction, adapted from a Margaret Mitchell novel, was seen by tens of millions of Americans and remains the highest-grossing film in history, adjusted for inflation.
The first long-distance limited-access highway in the U.S. opened between the Pennsylvania towns of Irwin and Carlisle, serving as a model for the future interstate highway system.

"A date which will live in infamy," President Franklin D. Roosevelt said of the day the Japanese military conducted a surprise strike on the U.S. naval base in Oʻahu, killing 2,403. Now forced to join World War II after staying neutral, America and its allies would win the war four years later.

A Japanese submarine struck coastal targets near Santa Barbara in the first shelling of the American mainland during World War II, influencing President Franklin D. Roosevelt's decision to incarcerate Japanese Americans during the war.

Part of the Manhattan Project, the X-10 Graphite Reactor at the Oak Ridge National Laboratory was the world's first artificial nuclear reactor designed for continuous operation and the production of plutonium.
A massive explosion caused by the explosion of munitions being loaded onto a World War II cargo vessel killed 320 people, mostly Black sailors, leading to a mutiny trial and naval desegregation.

The world's first detonation of a nuclear weapon took place in the desolate Jornada del Muerto desert, releasing the explosive energy of 25 kilotons of TNT. Three weeks later, a nuclear weapon would be used for the first time in war, with the American atomic bombing of Hiroshima, Japan.
The first general-purpose electronic digital computer was formally dedicated at the University of Pennsylvania, marking a revolutionary step in the history of computing.

The July 8, 1947 issue of the Roswell Daily Record announced the capture of a flying saucer on a ranch near a New Mexico town. The U.S. Army reported that the "flying saucer" was merely a weather balloon, but conspiracy theories of extraterrestrial spacecraft would continue to flourish.

Incumbent President Harry S. Truman, passing through St. Louis, had his picture taken with an early edition of the Chicago Daily Tribune, two days after the 1948 presidential election. "Dewey Defeats Truman," the headline read—but Truman had just won re-election in a stunning upset.
Jim Roper won the inaugural race of the Strictly Stock Series, driving a 1949 Lincoln 200 laps around a 0.75-mile dirt oval. Today, NASCAR is one of America's most popular spectator sports.

In a speech to the Republican Women's Club of Wheeling, Wisconsin senator Joseph McCarthy claimed to have a list of 205 known communists working for the State Department. None of his suspects were found guilty, but McCarthy set off a second "Red Scare" in America as concerns over Soviet espionage intensified.
928 nuclear tests were conducted at Frenchman Flat over the course of four decades during the Cold War, creating mushroom clouds visible from Las Vegas.
Dr. Jonas Salk and a team at the University of Pittsburgh developed the first successful vaccine against polio, a major medical breakthrough that saved countless lives.
Chevrolet began production of its iconic sports car, which became a symbol of American style and automotive performance.

In 1951, Topeka's public school system refused to enroll a Black student at her nearest school, instead requiring her to ride a bus to a segregated school. Based on this case, the Supreme Court ruled that racial segregation in public schools was unconstitutional, overturning the "separate but equal" doctrine.

Rosa Parks, a Black woman, was arrested for her refusal to surrender her seat to a White person, sparking a year-long boycott of the Montgomery public transit system that became a foundational event of the Civil Rights Movement.

properties.event: Construction begins on the Interstate Highway System
properties.description: President Dwight D. Eisenhower signed an act authorizing the construction of a massive national highway network, transforming American travel and centering the automobile in the American identity. On August 13, work began on what is now Interstate 70 in eastern Missouri.
Federal troops were sent to enforce the integration of Central High School after Arkansas Orval Faubus attempted to block Black students, known as the "Little Rock Nine," from entering.
The first U.S. satellite was successfully launched into orbit, marking America's official entry into the Space Race.
A tragic plane crash killed rock and roll stars Buddy Holly, Ritchie Valens, and "The Big Bopper" J. P. Richardson, a profound loss for the music world.
Black students staged nonviolent protests at a Woolworth's lunch counter, sparking a wave of sit-ins across the South to end racial segregation.

Civil rights activists known as the "Freedom Riders" testing desegregation laws on interstate buses were brutally attacked and their bus firebombed by a White mob, while the police and the FBI stood aside.

properties.event: James Meredith enrolls at Ole Miss
properties.description: James Meredith became the first Black student to enroll at the University of Mississippi, requiring protection from thousands of federal troops. He would graduate the next year with a degree in political science.

President John F. Kennedy was shot and killed while traveling in a motorcade, a traumatic event that stunned the nation and the world. Vice President Lyndon B. Johnson was sworn in as president two hours later.
Civil rights activists James Chaney, Andrew Goodman, and Michael Schwerner were murdered by members of the Ku Klux Klan while working to register Black voters in Mississippi.

State troopers brutally attacked peaceful marchers crossing the Edmund Pettus Bridge in Selma, galvanizing national support for the Voting Rights Act, which would prohibit racial discrimination in voting.
Three years after Ernesto Miranda's arrest by the Phoenix Police Department, the Supreme Court ruled that law enforcement must inform a person of their constitutional rights before interrogation.

America's bloodiest riot in 100 years, the 12th Street Riot was the climax of the "long, hot summer of 1967" across the country, following racial tensions caused by police brutality and housing discrimination.

properties.event: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.
properties.description: Civil rights leader Martin Luther King Jr. was assassinated, leading to nationwide grief and riots in many American cities. Today, a federal holiday is celebrated in King's honor each January.

"One small step for [a] man, one giant leap for mankind," said Neil Armstrong upon taking his first step onto the surface of the Moon, a historic achievement for America and for all of humanity. The Apollo 11 mission was overseen from the Mission Control Center in Houston.

National Guard troops fired on students protesting the Vietnam War, killing four and wounding nine. Millions of students would demonstrate across the country following the shootings, fueling sentiment against the war.

Military analyst Daniel Ellsberg surrendered to federal authorities in Boston after leaking documents related to the U.S. government's involvement in the Vietnam War, which were published in The New York Times and revealed extensive deception on the part of Lyndon B. Johnson's administration.

A break-in at the Democratic National Committee headquarters at the Watergate complex, conducted by members of President Richard Nixon's 1972 re-election campaign operation, led to a massive political scandal and Nixon's eventual resignation.
Now known as the Willis Tower, the Sears Tower still crowns the Chicago skyline as the world's tallest building from its construction to 1998, with a height of 1,451 feet.

The 19-year-old granddaughter of publishing magnate William Randolph Hearst was kidnapped from her Berkeley apartment by the Symbionese Liberation Army, a small far-left militant organization, later conducting a bank robbery in what became a national media sensation.

properties.event: Camp Pendleton opens to Vietnamese refugees
properties.description: As the Vietnam War came to an end, Marine Corps Base Camp Pendleton became the point of arrival for over 50,000 refugees as part of the largest humanitarian airlift in history.

President Gerald Ford honored the conclusion of the Bicentennial Wagon Train Pilgrimage, a convoy of covered wagons crossing the country using the original pioneer trails, commemorating America's 200th birthday.

Over 2,000 protesters, part of the Clamshell Alliance, occupied the construction site of a nuclear power plant in New Hampshire. 1,414 were taken into custody in one of the largest mass arrests in American history.

properties.event: Federal emergency declared at Love Canal
properties.description: President Jimmy Carter declared a federal health emergency in a Niagara Falls neighborhood where a landfill caused major exposure to toxic chemicals. The disaster led to the creation of the national Superfund environmental remediation program.

The Unit 2 reactor of the Three Mile Island Nuclear Generating Station suffered a partial meltdown, releasing radioactive gases into the atmosphere. The accident came at severe cost to public confidence in nuclear energy, shutting down new construction for decades.
"Do you believe in miracles? Yes!" The U.S. men's hockey team defeated the heavily favored Soviet team 4-3 at the Winter Olympics, a historic and inspiring sports victory.

President Ronald Reagan was shot in the chest after speaking at the Washington Hilton hotel. When First Lady Nancy Reagan arrived at his bedside, the president's first words were: "Honey, I forgot to duck." President Reagan made a speedy recovery.
Surgeons at the University of Utah successfully implanted the Jarvik-7, the first permanent artificial heart, in patient Barney Clark.

America's most active volcano began an eruption in January 1983 that did not cease until September 2018, destroying buildings and highways and creating hundreds of acres of new land as lava flowed into the ocean.

The most financially successful modern Olympics saw a boycott from fourteen Eastern Bloc countries in response to the American-led boycott of the 1980 Moscow Olympics. The United States won 83 gold medals, by far the most of any country.
8-seed Villanova defeated defending national champions Georgetown in a stunning upset to win the first 64-team NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament, known as "March Madness."

Three months after the tragic explosion of Space Shuttle Challenger off the coast of Florida, the remains of the seven astronauts, including schoolteacher Christa McAuliffe, were returned to Dover Air Force Base.

NBC, CBS, and ABC all interrupted their primetime programming to broadcast the rescue of "Baby Jessica" McClure, an 18-month-old who was freed 58 hours after falling into her aunt's well in West Texas.
Cornell graduate student Robert Tappan Morris launched an early malware program from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology network "to see if it could be done," receiving a felony conviction.
An oil supertanker ran aground, spilling over 10 million gallons of crude oil into Prince William Sound in one of the worst environmental disasters in American history.
In the largest art heist in history, thieves stole 13 works of art worth hundreds of millions of dollars. The case remains unsolved, and the artworks remain missing.

As America's butter surplus reached its peak, the U.S. government purchased excess cheese and butter to keep prices stable, leaving mountains of dairy sitting in warehouses. At a Wisconsin cold storage facility, an eight-day fire turned 10 to 15 million pounds of butter into a literal river of fat.
Widespread civil unrest broke out following the acquittal of police officers filmed beating Rodney King, resulting in dozens of deaths in the most destructive local unrest in American history.
A 51-day standoff between federal agents and a religious cult known as the Branch Davidians ended in a fire that killed 76 people, including many children.
The first broadcast of a radio signal over the internet was achieved by WXYC at the University of North Carolina, marking the beginning of the streaming era.
An anti-government extremist detonated a truck bomb that destroyed the Alfred P. Murrah Federal Building, killing 168 people in the deadliest act of homegrown terrorism in American history.

A domestic terrorist committed a pipe bombing in downtown Atlanta during the Summer Olympics, killing two and wounding over 100. The games continued as planned, marking the 100th anniversary of the 1896 Summer Olympics in Athens.
A Korean Air Boeing 747 crashed on approach to Guam's international airport, resulting in the deaths of 229 people on board.

Computer scientists Larry Page and Sergey Brin launched the world's most popular search engine from their friend Susan Wojcicki's garage in Menlo Park, fundamentally changing how the world accessed information.

Two students killed 12 classmates and a teacher at Columbine High School, sparking a national debate on gun control and school safety. In the years since, dozens of copycat attacks have been inspired by the shooting.
A disputed presidential election in Florida led to a weeks-long recount and a Supreme Court decision that awarded the presidency to George W. Bush over Al Gore.

Terrorists from the Islamist group al-Qaeda hijacked four planes, two of which were flown into the Twin Towers of the World Trade Center. Nearly 3,000 people were killed in what remains the deadliest terrorist attack in history.
Just five months after the September 11 attacks, President George W. Bush opened the Olympic Games in Utah's capital. The Winter Olympics will return to Salt Lake City in 2034.
Space Shuttle Columbia disintegrated as it re-entered the atmosphere over Texas and Louisiana, killing all seven crew members and leading to the temporary grounding of the shuttle fleet.
Mark Zuckerberg launched "TheFacebook" from his Harvard dorm, starting a social media platform that would come to have billions of users.
A powerful hurricane and the failure of New Orleans' levee system caused catastrophic flooding and $125 billion of damage along the Gulf Coast.
Dick Cheney became the second U.S. vice president to shoot someone while in office, although in this case it was an accident during a quail hunt. Harry Whittington would survive the incident.

Apple co-founder Steve Jobs launched the most transformative technology of the 21st century, with the company officially releasing the first mainstream smartphone in June 2007. The internet could now fit in your pocket.
The largest bankruptcy filing in U.S. history, involving over $600 billion in assets, triggered a global financial crisis and the deepest recession since the 1930s.
Captain Chesley "Sully" Sullenberger successfully ditched US Airways Flight 1549 in the Hudson River after a bird strike, saving the lives of all 155 people aboard.

An April 20 explosion on an offshore oil rig caused the largest marine oil spill in history, devastating the Gulf of Mexico's ecosystem. On May 6, the oil washed ashore for the first time, on the barrier islands of the Louisiana coast.
A massive EF5 tornado struck the city of Joplin, killing 158 people and becoming the costliest single tornado in American history.
A gunman killed 20 children and six adults at an elementary school, leading to renewed calls for national gun safety reforms.
Two Islamist domestic terrorists planted two pressure cooker bombs near the finish line of the famous race, killing three and wounding hundreds. A four-day manhunt would follow.

"Hands up, don't shoot." The fatal police shooting of an unarmed Black teenager sparked weeks of protests and brought national attention to issues of racial profiling, fueling the "Black Lives Matter" movement.
A white supremacist killed nine Black parishioners during a Bible study, sparking the widespread removal of Confederate symbols in the South.

Protests led by the Standard Rock Sioux Tribe against a pipeline project drew thousands of supporters to protect water and tribal lands. On November 20, over 300 protesters were injured by the actions of North Dakota law enforcement officers.

A group of white nationalists and neo-Nazis held a rally in protest of the planned removal of a Confederate statue. The rally turned violent when a self-proclaimed white supremacist rammed his car into a crowd of counter-protesters, killing one person and injuring 19 others.

The deadliest and most destructive wildfire in California history nearly destroyed the town of Paradise, as well as Concow, Magalia, and Butte Creek Canyon. The fire was caused by a power line failure during high winds.
A domestic terrorist driven by anti-Hispanic sentiment killed 23 people and injured 22 others at a Walmart store in El Paso.
The death of a Black man at the hands of a police officer sparked global protests for racial justice and widespread demands for police reform.
Supporters of President Donald Trump stormed the U.S. Capitol in an attempt to disrupt the certification of the 2020 presidential election.

Artificial intelligence company OpenAI released a chatbot called ChatGPT, which reached 100 million users in just two months. ChatGPT's release supercharged the global AI boom, which shows no signs of slowing down.
Fast-moving wildfires destroyed much of the historic town of Lahaina, resulting in the deadliest wildfire in modern U.S. history.

Then-former President Donald Trump was shot in his ear by a sniper while speaking at a campaign rally. Trump would accept the Republican nomination the following week and go on to become the second president in American history to win two nonconsecutive terms.
Right-wing political activist Charlie Kirk was shot and killed while speaking at a campus debate, in the most recent high-profile instance of increasing political violence in America in the 2020s.
